# The Future of Chemical Manufacturing in 2025: Innovation, Sustainability, and Digital Transformation

**The Future of Chemical Manufacturing in 2025: Innovation, Sustainability, and Digital Transformation**

As we venture deeper into the decade, chemical manufacturing in 2025 is transforming with a focus on innovation, sustainability, and digital integration. Modern facilities are increasingly embracing sustainable chemical production and green chemistry practices that not only minimize environmental impact but also optimize operational efficiency.

One of the most significant trends reshaping the industry is its commitment to sustainability. Companies across the globe are investing in energy efficiency measures and responsible water management strategies that position them as leaders in sustainable chemical production. This shift is driven by a collective desire to reduce emissions and waste, supporting a cleaner and greener future while maintaining high standards of industrial chemical production.

Digital transformation in chemical manufacturing is accelerating at a rapid pace. State-of-the-art technologies such as advanced analytics, machine learning, and automation are revolutionizing the way chemical processing operates. From predictive maintenance facilitated by real-time process monitoring to the use of digital twins in process control, the benefits of automation in chemical manufacturing are unmistakable. These digital tools not only enhance product quality and process efficiency but also fortify safety measures across operations.

Innovation is the driving force behind the evolution of chemical processing. High-throughput experimentation and advanced process control are leading to breakthroughs in specialty chemicals and environmentally friendly products. Companies are utilizing cutting-edge methodologies to develop high-performance materials that meet the demanding needs of modern industries while upholding sustainability standards.

Safety and regulatory compliance remain at the forefront of these advancements. Chemical manufacturers are leveraging data-driven risk management techniques that ensure stringent safety protocols are followed, minimizing the possibility of workplace incidents. This focus on safety, combined with improved compliance measures, helps companies maintain operational resilience and secure their position in global chemical production trends.

Moreover, the global chemical supply chain is undergoing a significant transformation. With an increasing reliance on advanced logistics and integrated systems, streamlined chemical logistics are making it possible to connect supply chain operations worldwide. This enhances efficiency and responsiveness, allowing companies to better manage costs and adapt to changes in global demand.

Looking ahead, the future of chemical manufacturing in 2025 is marked by the convergence of sustainable practices, digital transformation, and relentless innovation. As companies continue to push the boundaries of technological integration and operational excellence, the industry is set to achieve new heights in sustainable and safe production. Embracing these trends will not only ensure the vitality of the chemical sector but also pave the way for a cleaner, brighter future in industrial chemical production.

Digital twins in chemical engineering: An integrated framework for identification, implementation, online learning, and uncertainty assessment
==============================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Carine Menezes Rebello, Idelfonso B.R.Nogueira

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.compchemeng.2025.109178](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.compchemeng.2025.109178 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0098135425001826&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •A digital twin framework integrates AI for real-time process monitoring and prediction. 
*   •Uncertainty management enhances digital twin reliability in dynamic environments. 
*   •Gas lift and ESP case studies validate the framework’s adaptive learning approach. 
*   •Online learning and transfer learning improve model accuracy over time. 
*   •Statistical model reduction optimizes computational efficiency for real-time use. 

Abstract
--------

A digital representation of a cyber–physical system (CPS) offers significant opportunities, containing dynamic process tracking in real-time, inferring parameters, and enabling constant learning to refine insights and predictive capabilities. This concept, referred to as a digital twin (DT), is increasingly applied in multiple sectors of industries but faces distinct issues depending on the application. In chemical engineering, for example, discrepancies between physical processes and models often arise due to unobservable state changes, while the complexity of these systems demands simplifications that introduce uncertainties. In addition, resource constraints in industrial environments further complicate implementation. This study presents a comprehensive framework for developing and implementing DTs in process units, with applications demonstrated in a gas-lift system and an electrical submersible pump (ESP). The framework incorporates techniques such as online learning, transfer learning, Bayesian inference, and Monte Carlo simulations, along with innovative methods such as dimensionality reduction and enhanced cognitive strategies to ensure adaptive capabilities. By addressing gaps in uncertainty management and integrating advanced learning methodologies, this framework supports the creation of reliable and flexible DTs, capable of operating efficiently in dynamic and complex environments.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001760)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001929)

Keywords
--------

Digital twin

Artificial intelligence

Uncertainty

Transfer learning

Online learning

1. Introduction
---------------

Developing a virtual replica of a cyber–physical system (CPS) introduces many opportunities. One such opportunity is the ability to model the CPS within an extensive digital environment, allowing simultaneous real-time evaluations of both the physical system and its digital equivalent. This system ensures accurate and trustworthy information about the real-world context by persistently gathering insights from the physical process. Often referred to as the “digital twin” or “twinning process”, this methodology can be interpreted as a type of virtual duplication. Initially introduced by Michael Grieves and John Vickers in a NASA presentation in 2017 ([Grieves and Vickers, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b21)), NASA emerged as an early adopter of the digital twin concept, particularly within the realm of space exploration.

A DT aims to establish a dynamic, two-way real-time connection between the physical world and its digital representation. This idea has progressively gained momentum across various industrial sectors and is now widely seen as an important technology for future industries. [Ganguli and Adhikari, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b16), [Qi et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b48), [Jones et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b27), [Costa et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b13), [Moreira et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b42), [Rebello et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b54), [Queiroz et al., 2023b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b50), [Ghobakhloo, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b18), [Cañas et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b9) and [Yuan et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b70). Following 2017, publications addressing the digital twin concept have significantly increased. [Jones et al. (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b27), With a particular focus on assessing the manufacturing process and product lifecycle. More recently, DTs have also begun to make inroads into the process systems engineering field.

However, the notion of digital twins evolves as it diffuses within process system engineering (PSE) context. This natural evolution requires clarification of misconceptions that have emerged. In a recent critique, [Grieves (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b20) has emphasized that “digital model” (DM), “digital shadow” (DS), and “digital twin” are not simply subcategories of one another. The classification of DM, DS, and DT based solely on the nature (manual or automatic) of data flow between physical and virtual entities leads to logical inconsistencies, infinite regressions, and a return to siloed function-specific viewpoints. Instead, Grieves reaffirms the intended role of digital twins as constructs that holistically integrate both physical and virtual environments, transcending simplistic data-exchange taxonomies. This perspective encourages distinguishing an isolated, static construct used, for example, in design feasibility studies from a DT, which actively evolves alongside its physical counterpart, as we present in this work and questions the usefulness of the DS category altogether.

Adopting a clarified definition of DT remains a challenge within the process systems engineering domain [Negri et al. (2017)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b43) and [Emmert-Streib (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b15). A key issue is the need to dynamically solve complex numerical problems, often governed by partial differential equations, while maintaining an adaptive, learning-based connection to the operational plant. This complexity underscores the importance of establishing a robust and conceptually sound framework for DTs, ensuring they serve as genuinely transformative tools rather than superficial or misapplied buzzwords.

Within the realm of digital twins, AI plays a role in facilitating the modeling and representation, alongside the learning of complex behaviors, interactions among system components and data and active learning from its physical counterpart ([Huang et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b26), [Reis and Saraiva, 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b60), [Han et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b22), [Mesbah and Graves, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b38), [Yuan et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b71)). Although first principles models may yield comparable results, the significant computational resources required to compute numerical solutions for these models, coupled with the need for adaptability, make your application unviable for exchanging information in real-time. [Schweidtmann et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b63). In contrast, artificial intelligence models provide the benefit of ongoing learning from the process, enabling the CPS to adapt. This method, known as online learning, is an effective approach that minimizes the computing demand required to operate an AI model in real-time.

Consequently, an increasing need arises for studies focused on advancing and integrating AI and digital twins. [Schweidtmann et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b63). Nevertheless, incorporating adaptive learning methods into the framework raises computing resource requirements, which scale with the frequency of its activation. As a result, efficient resource management in those contexts is necessary to maintain the advantages that surrogate models provide ([Alizadeh et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b2), [Michalopoulou and Papathanasiou, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b39)). This issue is more prominent in situations addressing the AI model uncertainty forecasts, as it usually requires numerous assessments of the parameter’s probability distribution, which can increase the computational effort.

Machine learning (ML) models have gradually become widespread in a variety of fields for tasks involving decision-making and inference ([Nogueira et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b44), [Rebello et al., 2022a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b55), [Queiroz et al., 2023a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b49), [Wang et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b67), [Wang et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b66), [Dobbelaere et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b14), [Savage et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b62)). Nevertheless, in order to implement AI techniques effectively, a thorough assessment of their reliability and performance is needed. The model outputs can be influenced by noise and inaccuracies in the methods used for both modeling and inference ([Rebello et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b53)). Consequently, it is important to account for AI models’ uncertainty and potential drawbacks when relying on their predictions to guide decisions ([Almeida Costa et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b3), [Tomsett et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b65), [Kläs and Vollmer, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b28)). Thus, accurately capturing and representing uncertainty in any AI-driven system is important.

From this perspective, and in line with what was discussed earlier regarding digital twins, there is an increasing focus on creating models beyond computational efficiency, incorporating robustness, adaptability, and some level of cognitive capability. Rather than simply acting as static digital representations or basic data mirrors, these advanced digital twins should be able to self-adapt when discrepancies happen among predictions and observed measurements. In this context, the digital twin needs to be prepared to address the dynamic complexities inherent in numerous process industries.

Tackling the demand for robustness has become an increasingly prominent subject in academic discussions, particularly within the area of AI-enhanced Cyber–Physical Systems ([Sakhnini and Karimipour, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b61), [Moosavi et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b41), [Radanliev et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b52), [Mavikumbure et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b36)). For example, [Costa et al. (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b12) studied a robust learning approach for uncertainty-aware scientific machine learning models, addressing epistemic uncertainties, data corruption, incomplete understanding of underlying physical principles, and substantial computational limitations. By applying this approach to developing soft sensors in a polymerization process, they illustrated how such adaptive and uncertainty-driven techniques can strengthen model resilience. Similarly, [Gneiting et al. (2007)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b19) put forward a method for calibrating the distribution of a known random variable, emphasizing the need to manage nondeterministic factors properly to obtain reliable AI predictions. However, these studies generally assume offline settings with abundant computational resources, which often contrasts with the demands of real-time industrial applications, where both efficiency and flexibility are required.

Parallel efforts in this field of optimization have pointed to the importance of robustness in optimization problems and have introduced novel methodologies that extend the usual deterministic multiobjective and single-object optimizations to more comprehensive optimal or Pareto regions with statistical confidence ([Rebello et al., 2021a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b57), [Rebello et al., 2021b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b58)). Recent work has emphasized the growing importance of gaussian process (GP) modeling for robust, adaptive process optimization and control in dynamic settings. One approach integrates adversarial robustness with adaptive GP learning to manage both control-layer disturbances and plant-model mismatch ([Ahmed et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b1), [Bradford et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b7)), while another showcases how GPs can capture predictive uncertainty in complex chemical processes to guide efficient, sustainable operations ([Bradford et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b7)). These efforts in the field of optimization have been promoting solutions and tools to address the uncertainty quantification in the optimization and control of chemical processes ([Lucia et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b33)). Carrying these efforts to online modeling and DTs is a natural step towards reliable machine-supported decision-making.

Recognizing and quantifying the uncertainty of the model prediction is particularly valuable in operational settings. This can help decision-makers better gauge reliability, identify conditions that lead to poor performance, and determine when additional data or model improvements are needed. This perspective is important for CPS applications in complex and risk-sensitive chemical processes. For example, economic, safety, and environmental considerations demand robust and reliable digital twins in scenarios where operational parameters may vary widely, product quality must be closely managed, and safety margins are critical. This characterizes several sectors of the chemical industry. For example, the polymerization process, where the polymer properties (density and melt flow index) determine what type of products are being produced ([Nogueira and Pontes, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b45), [Rácz et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b51), [Nogueira et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b47)); the pharmaceutical industry ([Bauer et al., 1998](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b4), [Sokolov et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b64)), where the purity of the final product should be strictly addressed ([Lima et al., 2025](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b32)); oil and gas sector exploration, which demands careful economic, safety, and environmental considerations ([Cordes et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b10), [Martins et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b34), [Mojarad et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b40)).

Despite a surge of interest in digital twins in chemical engineering, a gap persists: the literature rarely provides a truly integrated strategy for handling complexity, uncertainty, and continuous adaptation in these systems. Most existing works consider key elements — such as robust learning, transfer learning, or online updates — in isolation and thus fail to realize their full potential as an interdependent ecosystem ([Costa et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#b13)). To address this shortcoming, the present work proposes a cohesive, concept-driven digital twin framework for chemical engineering that harmonizes these methods into a unified, self-reinforcing cycle of inference, adaptation, and uncertainty awareness. Validated through two experimentally tested case studies, gas lift and electrical submersible pump (ESP), our framework is not simply a collection of techniques but a carefully orchestrated approach designed to ensure that each step feeds into and strengthens the next.

This framework is conceptually structured as follows:

*   1._Offline training_: Establishes a rich foundational model, providing a robust baseline before deployment and ensuring that subsequent adaptations occur on solid ground. 
*   2._Bayesian inference_: Enriches the model with a principled understanding of parameter uncertainties, informing downstream decisions about when and how to adjust. 
*   3._Monte Carlo simulations_: Operationalizes uncertainty assessments, translating abstract probability distributions into actionable insights about prediction reliability. 
*   4._Transfer learning_: Ensures the model’s knowledge remains relevant as it migrates to new operational environments, preserving continuity and adaptation across domains. 
*   5._Model space reduction with statistical confidence_: Streamlines computational complexity, empowering the framework to remain agile and reactive under constrained conditions. 
*   6._Cognitive tack_: Imbues the digital twin with a higher-level awareness of its own performance and data context, enabling more strategic and targeted interventions. 
*   7._Online learning_: Closes the loop by continuously refining the model’s structure, ensuring that recognized drifts in operating conditions are promptly and effectively countered. 

These elements function as interlocking components of a single conceptual system. Instead of standing alone, each technique informs and improves the others, forming an integrated framework that is more than the sum of its parts. This synergy enables digital twins to dynamically evolve in response to uncertainties and shifting conditions, ultimately providing a more reliable, adaptive, and insightful foundation for decision-making in complex industrial settings.

2. Methodology
--------------

### 2.1. Offline identification

Offline identification is the first methodological step in the development of the DT, serving as the foundation for its implementation. In this phase, historical data and robust computational resources are utilized to train machine learning models, enabling them to capture patterns and system complexities precisely. Initially, data is systematically collected, considering relevant variables, using design of experiments (DoE) or process data (Section [2.1.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#sec2.1.1)). The latin hypercube sampling (LHS) method was applied to this study, ensuring a uniform distribution in the operational space. Subsequently, the nonlinear autoregressive with exogenous inputs (NARX) structure was employed, and it is known for its effectiveness in modeling dynamic systems. The definition of lag dimensions was performed using the Lipschitz coefficient, ensuring that the model structure adequately captured the system’s dynamics (Section [2.1.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#sec2.1.2)). The HYPERBAND algorithm was employed to optimize the model’s hyperparameters, such as learning rate, number of dense layers, and activation functions, reducing computational costs, maximizing performance, and supporting the training and validation of neural networks (NN) (Section [2.1.3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#sec2.1.3)). Finally, parameter uncertainty assessment was conducted using Bayesian inference, Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), and Monte Carlo (MC) methods, ensuring robust predictions and informing future adjustment decisions (Section [2.1.4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#sec2.1.4)). [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098135425001826#fig1) illustrates the overall flow of the nonlinear model identification strategy, detailing the steps of data collection, model structure selection, and hyperparameter tuning, showcasing how these methodologies integrate to provide a solid foundation for the digital twin.

#### 2.1.1. Data acquisition

The initial phase in developing artificial neural networks involves gathering data, which may originate from real process measurements, synthetic data generated by the virtual plant, or a composition of both. To ensure that the network operates effectively across various conditions, a substantial volume of data must be collected. Moreover, incorporating extreme cases and boundary conditions enhances the model’s capacity for robustness and generalization.

A virtual plant can be used to generate simulated data in a software-in-the-loop (SIL) strategy. Thus, this plant serves as an environment for the digital twin that replicates the process behavior in real time.

During the data acquisition phase, it is necessary to define which inputs are appropriate for the model. Ensuring that these inputs remain independent of cross-correlations is i

enriched_points.point_title: Digital twins as a game changer
enriched_points.expanded_content: Digital twins revolutionize chemical manufacturing by enabling real-time monitoring and predictive analytics, enhancing operational efficiency, safety management, and process optimization across the industry.

Proactive strategies facilitated by digital twins allow for predictive maintenance and operational adjustments, minimizing downtime and improving responsiveness to market demands and environmental regulations.

enriched_points.point_title: Vision for a resilient chemical manufacturing landscape
enriched_points.expanded_content: The integration of digital twins fosters a resilient chemical manufacturing landscape, driving sustainability, continuous innovation, and adaptability in response to evolving industry challenges and opportunities.
